Edexcel GCSE English Language Paper 2 Section B — 10 Most Likely Questions + Skeleton Plans (2026)

For: Edexcel (Pearson) GCSE English Language, Paper 2 (1EN0/02), Section B. You answer ONE task — Question 8 (EITHER) or Question 9 (OR). The two are a choice of form and topic, not sub-parts of one task. 40 marks — AO5 (Communication and Organisation) 24 marks; AO6 (Technical Accuracy) 16 marks. Target on the page: 650+ words. Under that risks an AO5 penalty.

Source. The themes below are Sophicly's verified 2026 forecast for 1EN0/02 Section B (see The Complete Writer's Craft — Creative Non-Fiction, Verified Past-Paper Writing Prompts section, for the full table of statements, forms, audiences, and paired reading sources). The forecast applies recurrence-cycle analysis to Edexcel's own cluster pattern plus current-affairs salience for a 2026 candidate. Edexcel's Section A pairs a 19th-century non-fiction text with a 20th/21st-century one, and the two reading texts almost always prime the Section B writing theme — so the cluster that surfaces in the reading is the cluster most likely to surface in the writing.

Statistics and sources. Every numeric claim and named source cited below has been verified against UK government, regulator, charity, or peer-reviewed sources (Ofcom, NHS, Met Office, WWF, WRAP, UNEP, UNWTO, IFS, Mental Health Foundation, and similar). Examiners penalise invented statistics; everything here is real and citeable in exam conditions. Where a theme needs a number this document could not verify, the claim is marked [VERIFY-NEEDED] — never state an unverified figure as fact.

Variety of openings — no two model answers start the same way. A Grade-9 answer is not just a correctly structured IUMVCC; it opens in a way that is creative and fits the topic. There is no single "correct" opening. The model essays below deliberately use a different hook archetype each time, so you can see the range available to you — and so the document never teaches one formula. The hooks Sophicly teaches: a question, an "imagine" scenario, a metaphor, an anecdote, a shocking statistic, a bold statement, a contrast, an assumption-then-reversal, repetition. Pick whichever genuinely suits the statement Edexcel prints — not whichever you rehearsed. Forcing a clever opening onto the wrong topic loses more than it gains; the opening must earn its place.

Opening-hook map — what each model answer demonstrates (so you see the full range):

Q Topic Hook archetype used What it demonstrates
Q1 Volunteering / community Personal anecdote A two-sentence concrete memory that earns the argument
Q2 Technology / screen time Extended metaphor (the topic as a machine) A long controlled sentence that builds one central image
Q3 Climate / individual action Shocking statistic A single verified number that overturns assumption
Q4 Family / generations Historical contrast (then vs now) Two eras placed side by side to expose what changed
Q5 Travel / responsible tourism "Imagine" scenario A future moment the reader is asked to picture
Q6 Money / consumerism Bold statement A claim made with no qualifier
Q7 Mental health / pressure Extended metaphor (a second one) The metaphor technique flexes onto a new topic
Q8 Animals / conservation Assumption set up, then reversed "You have heard… in fact the opposite is true"
Q9 Career / future Rhetorical question A question only the reader can answer
Q10 Discrimination / fairness Repetition / anaphora Rhythm as persuasion — "We measure. We compare. We exclude."

These are illustrations, not prescriptions. The same topic could open with a different archetype and score just as highly — provided the choice fits. Study the variety; copy the range, not any single example. Each essay still runs IUMVCC underneath. The architecture does not change; the opening does. That variety is what inventive structure (AO5 top band) rewards.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

  1. A predicted theme for Edexcel P2 Section B in 2026, with three plausible stem variants (mixing Q8-style and Q9-style framings).
  2. A frame — the controlling argument that holds your whole answer together.
  3. Three hook options so you can pick the archetype that suits the stem Edexcel actually prints.
  4. A skeleton IUMVCC plan with sentence-level guidance for every beat.
  5. Three counter-argument variants so the strongest objection the printed prompt invites is the one you concede.
  6. A voice and banned-patterns check to run before you submit.

The plan structure stays identical across all ten questions. You rehearse the shell once; on the day, you swap in the surface prompt's form, audience, and statement of opinion. This is the same discipline that powers your Literature answers — memorise the load-bearing structure, recombine for the printed question.

Full IUMVCC walkthrough: Sophicly Persuasive Writing Guide.

A note on Edexcel forms — and the signature "guide" task

Edexcel's Section B has historically asked for an article, a letter, a speech or talk, a review, and — its signature shape — a section for a guide or guidebook ("Write a section for a guide aimed at…"). The guide form trips students who have only rehearsed articles, because a guide is written to help the reader do something, not only to persuade them to agree. The fix is small: keep IUMVCC underneath, but let the M (Methodology) beats read as genuine, usable guidance — the three things the reader should actually do — and let the V (Vision) beat paint what their life looks like once they have done them. Two of the ten model answers below are written to a guide task so you can see the register.

Whatever the form, the structure underneath is the same six beats. Match the register to the form Edexcel prints; do not change the architecture.

Memorise this once — the IUMVCC plan shape

Sophicly's transactional persuasive structure has SIX beats. Memorise the beat name, the word target, and the dominant move for each one.

Beat Words Sentences Dominant move
I — Introduction 50–100 2–3 Hook + topic bridge. No facts list. Build an image.
U — Urgency 100–150 3–4 Metaphor-first. Why this matters NOW.
M — Methodology 250–350 9–12 (three sub-points) Each sub-point: point, metaphor, development, evidence. Organic transitions only.
V — Vision 100–150 3 Three escalating sentences painting the better future.
C — Counter-argument 75–100 2–3 Concede the strongest objection. Refute with one decisive pivot.
C — Conclusion 75–100 2–3 Circular echo of the Introduction image, or a climactic final picture.

Total on the page: ~650–850 words. AO5 reward = inventive structure that does not feel formulaic. AO6 reward = controlled vocabulary, sentence variety, accurate spelling, punctuation and grammar.

The eight Sophicly hooks (pick ONE per piece)

Edexcel reward variety; pick the archetype that suits the form and audience the stem specifies.

  1. Counterintuitive fact — a number that overturns assumption.
  2. Personal anecdote — a two-sentence concrete memory.
  3. Sensory snapshot — a single image written like a paragraph from a novel.
  4. Imagine scenario — Picture this: + a future or hypothetical moment.
  5. Rhetorical question — a question only the reader can answer.
  6. Bold statement — a claim, no qualifier.
  7. Contrast / paradox — two clauses that crash against each other.
  8. Extended metaphor — a single comparison the rest of the piece returns to.

MADFATHER'S CROPS — the floor, not the ceiling

You need two or three devices working together at every persuasive moment. Single-device sentences score low on AO5. MADFATHER'S CROPS is an aide-memoire — it lists the techniques you should never forget, not every technique you are allowed to use. It is a scoring framework, a set of tools, nothing more.

The MADFATHER'S CROPS aide-memoire:

  • M Metaphor / A Alliteration / D Direct address / F Facts + foreshadowing / A Assonance / T Triadic structure / H Hyperbole / E Emotive language / R Rhetorical questions / C Contrast / R Repetition / O Onomatopoeia / P Personification / S Simile + sibilance.

Beyond MADFATHER'S CROPS — the wider technique bank. Top-band AO5 answers reach for techniques the aide-memoire does not list. Learn the names and the shapes; use them where the topic invites them.

  • Anaphora — repeated word at the start of consecutive clauses ("We have the science. We have the funding. We have the time we are not using."). Repetition that builds pressure.
  • Epistrophe — repeated word at the END of consecutive clauses ("Reduce the bag. Repair the shelf. Refuse the bag.").
  • Polysyndeton — repeated "and" connecting items ("The kit and the strip and the seat and the ticket and the broadcast."). Slows the reader down; emphasises accumulation.
  • Asyndeton — list with the conjunctions deliberately removed ("Reduce, repair, refuse."). Speeds the reader up; emphasises compression.
  • Polysyllabic listing — a list of multi-syllable words for cumulative weight ("relentless, industrialised, unregulated, profitable.").
  • Parallelism — same grammatical shape repeated ("Move the bell. Move the calendar. Move the system."). Anaphora plus parallelism is a Grade-9 combination.
  • Antithesis — two ideas balanced against each other in matched grammar ("The factory pays the wage; the wage pays the worker; the worker pays the factory back in attention.").
  • Chiasmus — A-B-B-A reversal ("Homes built for families are now families built for homes.").
  • Zeugma — one verb governing two objects, one literal and one figurative ("She lost her phone, her sleep, and her concentration in a single term.").
  • Climax / cumulative listing — list ordered by escalating intensity ("The bedroom dim. The classroom drifting. The decade lost.").
  • Bathos — sudden drop from the elevated to the trivial. Use sparingly, and only when irony serves the argument.
  • Periodic sentence — the main point withheld until the end ("Despite the algorithms, the platforms, the engineered loneliness and the absent hours — childhood survives.").
  • Cumulative sentence — main point first, then escalating detail ("The phone is the problem — the phone, the platform behind it, the algorithm behind the platform, and the boardroom behind the algorithm.").
  • Anastrophe — inverted word order ("Long the country has waited.").
  • Aporia — feigned uncertainty as a rhetorical move ("What does one even say to a generation raised on filters?").
  • Apostrophe — direct address to an absent person, idea or object ("Reader, you have done your share. The bin is not your fault.").
  • Litotes — emphasis via understated negative ("This is not a small problem.").
  • Anadiplosis — last word of one clause becomes first word of the next ("We confess to the bin. The bin confesses to the lorry. The lorry confesses to the export ship.").

The point. A Grade-9 answer does not just use techniques — it picks the technique that fits the IDEA. Anaphora suits mounting pressure; asyndeton suits decisive action; chiasmus suits paradox; polysyndeton suits oppressive accumulation. Match the technique to the meaning.

Effects on the Reader — Sophicly's 4-fold framework

Every beat in IUMVCC has a target effect on the reader:

  • I — Focus. What should the reader notice first?
  • U — Feel. What emotion grips them now?
  • M — Think. What argument is reshaping their judgement?
  • V — Feel + Act. What better future moves them?
  • C (counter) — Think. You have heard the objection; here is why it fails.
  • C (conclusion) — Act. The call that lands.

Name the Effect you are pursuing at every beat. Plans that only chase Think (logic) lose AO5 marks for failing to move the reader.

Banned patterns — they cost AO5 and AO6 marks

  • Shows / showing / shown anywhere in the piece. Replace with illustrates, demonstrates, emphasises, reveals, exposes, presents, conveys, signals, enacts.
  • Abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to). Replace with a concrete image.
  • More than ten to be verbs across the piece (is / are / was / were). Use active verbs.
  • More than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.
  • Discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly … Secondly … Finally). Bridge with verbal connectors instead.
  • Triadic structures without parallel grammar.
  • Vague intensifiers (really, very, just, basically).
  • American register (move as a noun, gotten, trash). British English only.
  • Exclamation marks outside quoted statements.

Persuasive craft — principles, not quotas

This is creative writing. There are no numeric rules — no fixed number of rhetorical questions, no set quota of anecdotes, no device confined to particular beats. Aristotle warned against persuasion by "scattered persuasive factors" (technique by count); judge by craft instead. The principles below are how to judge a choice, never boxes to tick.

  • Integrate the three appeals (Aristotle): ethos (why trust the writer?), pathos (what should the reader feel?), logos (what is the actual reason?) — woven together, not one technique repeated.
  • Fit the piece to its audience and occasion: a speech to peers is not a letter to a Minister. Register is judged on AO5.
  • Concrete beats abstract: one specific image or named person moves a reader more than a general claim ("one boy, twelve, walks two hours for water", not "many people suffer").
  • Clarity is honesty (Orwell): prefer the short word, the active verb, the fresh image; cut every word that does no work.
  • Rhythm at the climax: repetition and three-part structures land hardest where the idea peaks — used by instinct, never to a count.
  • Call to action sits in the Conclusion. Make it specific (sign up for one Saturday a month, switch the bedroom for downstairs), not vague (make a change).

Q1 — Volunteering, community, charity

Q1 — Volunteering, community, charity

Why this is likely. Community and giving is one of Edexcel's most enduring Section B clusters — it suits every form the paper offers (a guide on getting involved, a speech to peers, a letter on a local cause). The reading texts pair cleanly: Edexcel's 19th-century archive is rich in Victorian philanthropy and social-reform writing, and 21st-century journalism on community resilience and the volunteering of younger people is abundant. A cluster that has been primed often in the reading is overdue in the writing.

Three plausible stems (Edexcel prints ONE of these shapes):

  1. Write a section for a guide aimed at teenagers, explaining why and how they should volunteer in their community.
  2. "Volunteering is for people with spare time. Teenagers have none." Write a letter to your local newspaper giving your views.
  3. "The strongest communities are not the richest. They are the ones where people show up for each other." Write a speech to deliver at a school assembly.

Frame. Volunteering is not charity handed downward; it is membership earned sideways. A community is not a place you live in — it is a thing you build by showing up, and the teenager who builds it is building the place they will grow into.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Personal anecdote: "The first Saturday I gave to the food bank, I expected to feel useful. I did not expect to be the one who left changed."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "Only 16 per cent of people in England now volunteer formally at least once a month — down from 23 per cent before the pandemic. The fabric of community is thinning, one dropped stitch at a time." (NCVO, UK Civil Society Almanac, 2024.)
  • Imagine scenario: "Picture your street if every household gave one hour a month. Now picture it if none of them did."
  • Topic bridge: a community is built sideways, by people who show up, not handed down by people who can afford to.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the community as a fabric thinning at the seams.
  • Metaphor: "Every act of volunteering is a stitch; pull enough of them and the fabric holds. Stop, and the cloth quietly unravels."
  • Extension: the lunch club with no driver; the library shelved by one retiree; the youth team folding for want of a coach.
  • Evidence: NCVO's UK Civil Society Almanac (2024) — formal monthly volunteering in England has fallen to 16%, from 23% before the pandemic, and has not recovered.
  • Devices: triadic (lunch club / library / youth team); short pivot ("This is not someone else's job.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Start small and local. Frame: the smallest unit of community is one repeated hour. Metaphor: "A community is built in fortnightly hours, not heroic gestures." Develop: the food bank shift, the litter pick, the reading-buddy scheme at the local primary. Evidence: one named accessible route (a school volunteering coordinator, a council volunteer hub).
  • M2 — Give the skill you already have. Frame: you do not need to be qualified; you need to be willing. Metaphor: "The thing you find easy is the thing someone else finds impossible." Develop: a teenager who is good at maths tutoring a younger pupil; a confident speaker helping at a community event. Evidence: the #iwill Movement (a partnership of Volunteering Matters, UK Youth and the National Lottery Community Fund) supports young people across the UK to take social action.
  • M3 — Make it a habit, not a one-off. Frame: the value is in the return, not the visit. Metaphor: "A garden is not made by one day's planting." Develop: the standing commitment; the same Saturday each month; the relationships that only form over time. Evidence: NCVO's Time Well Spent research finds that regular, sustained volunteering produces deeper benefit — for volunteer and community alike — than one-off involvement.
  • Transitions — verbal bridges only: The first hour is one step. The skill is the second. The habit is the third. Never Firstly / Secondly / Finally.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The lunch club has drivers."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The library reopens its doors; the youth team finds its coach again."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "The street holds."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Time objection: "Teenagers are overloaded with revision; volunteering is a luxury they cannot afford."
  • Tokenism objection: "Teenage volunteering is just CV-padding that helps no one."
  • Scale objection: "One person's hour cannot fix a community's problems."
  • Refutation pivot (works for all three): "One hour does not fix a community; ten thousand hours, given by ten thousand people who each thought theirs would not matter, is precisely what a community is."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: return to the first Saturday at the food bank.
  • New move: the volunteer who expected to give, and was given more.
  • Final sentence: "Give one hour. The community you build is the one you grow up inside."

Model answer — written to Stem 3 (speech for a school assembly, ~715 words) — opens with a personal-anecdote hook

[I — Introduction]

The first Saturday I gave to the food bank, I expected to feel useful. I did not expect to be the one who left changed. I had imagined I would be handing things to people who needed them, and that the gratitude would flow one way, downward, from me to them. Instead I spent three hours beside a retired bus driver named Pauline who had been doing this every Saturday for eleven years, and by the end of the morning I understood that I had not come to give a community something. I had come to join one.

[U — Urgency]

Here is the thing nobody tells you about a community: it is a fabric, and it is thinning at the seams. Every act of volunteering is a stitch. Pull enough of them and the cloth holds; stop, and it quietly unravels. The lunch club two streets from this school nearly closed last winter because it had no driver. The local library now opens on the strength of one retired teacher. The under-elevens football team folded in September because no parent could spare the Saturdays to coach it. This is not someone else's job. This is the seam, and we are the thread.

[M1 — Start small and local]

So start small, and start local. A community is built in fortnightly hours, not in heroic gestures. You do not need to found a charity; you need to turn up to one. A food-bank shift, a litter pick, an hour reading with a struggling six-year-old at the primary down the road — each of these is a route, and each of them is open to you through one email to the volunteering coordinator in the school office. The first hour is the hardest only because it is the first.

[M2 — Give the skill you already have]

Then give the skill you already have. You do not need to be qualified; you need to be willing. The thing you find easy is the thing someone else finds impossible. If you are good at maths, a younger pupil three years behind you is drowning in the topic you find dull. If you are confident on a stage, a community fundraiser needs exactly that confidence. The talent you take for granted is, to the right person, a lifeline.

[M3 — Make it a habit, not a one-off]

And make it a habit, not a one-off. A garden is not made by one day's planting. The value of volunteering does not live in the single Saturday that looks good in a photograph; it lives in the return, the same time each month, the relationship with Pauline that only formed because I came back. Episodic giving is a visit. Sustained giving is membership. Choose the second.

[V — Vision]

The lunch club has drivers. The library reopens its doors; the youth team finds its coach again. The food bank has a queue of volunteers, not a shortage. The street holds.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you are thinking, in good faith, that you are buried in revision and that volunteering is a luxury you cannot afford. I understand the fear. But one hour does not fix a community, and nobody is asking it to. Ten thousand hours, given by ten thousand people who each thought theirs would not matter, is precisely what a community is. Your hour is not a drop in the ocean. Your hour is the ocean, in the only form an ocean ever takes.

[C — Conclusion]

So I will end where I began, on that first Saturday, expecting to give something and discovering I had been given more. The community you imagine is the work of strangers is, in fact, waiting for you to become one of the strangers who does the work. Give one hour. Give it this month. The community you build is the one you will grow up inside.

Alternative stems. The same IUMVCC shell answers Stem 1 (a guide section for teenagers on why and how to volunteer — let the three M-beats become the three pieces of practical guidance) and Stem 2 (a letter to the local paper rejecting "teenagers have no spare time"). The anecdote hook suits a speech especially well; for the guide, open with an imagine-scenario or a measured definition instead.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Fabric-and-seam metaphor anchored I → U → V. No shows. No exclamation marks. All statistics verified (NCVO 2024).

Q2 — Modern technology, screen time, online life

Q2 — Modern technology, screen time, online life

Why this is likely. Technology and its grip on daily life is the most current of all the clusters, and Edexcel has not yet tested it directly in Section B. Salience is high — the Online Safety Act 2023, the Smartphone Free Childhood movement founded in 2024, Jonathan Haidt's The Anxious Generation (2024). Edexcel's 19th-century archive holds Victorian anxieties about the telegraph and the sensationalist press, which pair naturally with a 21st-century screen-time article.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "The smartphone is the most useful tool a teenager owns and the most dangerous. Both things are true at once." Write an article for a magazine giving your views.
  2. Write a section for a guide aimed at parents, advising them how to manage their teenager's screen time at home.
  3. "Adults complain that teenagers live online. They forget who handed them the phone." Write a speech for a sixth-form debate.

Frame. The phone did not invent distraction; it industrialised it. The Sophicly position is not anti-technology — it is anti-design-without-conscience. Every feature engineered for maximum attention is engineered against the developing teenage brain. The reform target is not the user; it is the design, and the household that can choose around it.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Extended metaphor: "The smartphone is a casino built to fit a pocket, open all night, and tuned to the one customer least able to walk away."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "Ofcom's 2024 research found children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online — almost half their waking life outside school."
  • Sensory snapshot: "Half-past midnight. The room is dark; the face is lit blue. The thumb keeps scrolling, because the thumb has forgotten the brain it belongs to."
  • Topic bridge: design that profits from attention is design that costs childhood.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the attention economy as a machine running in a child's pocket.
  • Metaphor: "Every notification is a handle pulled in a child's pocket, and every win is engineered to lose."
  • Extension: the small dopamine wins; the escalating spend of hours; the family table emptying of attention.
  • Evidence: Ofcom 2024 Children and Parents: Media Use and Attitudes (online-time data for 12–17 year-olds); the Online Safety Act 2023 as the first regulatory recognition of the problem.
  • Devices: triadic on the win / spend / loss; short pivot ("This is not entertainment.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Design, not user. Frame: regulation must target the engineers, not the children. Metaphor: "We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards." Develop: infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks. Evidence: the Online Safety Act 2023 and its duties on platform design.
  • M2 — School as sanctuary. Frame: classrooms protected from device intrusion outperform those that surrender. Metaphor: "A school without phones is a school where attention can breathe again." Develop: phone-free pilots; restored eye contact. Evidence: Smartphone Free Childhood (founded 2024).
  • M3 — The home as architect. Frame: parents are not the police; they are the architects of the home's attention. Metaphor: "A charging station downstairs is scaffolding, not punishment." Develop: the bedroom is for sleep, not the algorithm. Evidence: Jonathan Haidt, The Anxious Generation (2024).
  • Transitions: The design is one half. The classroom is the second. The home is the third.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The bedside table holds books."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Family meals stretch into conversations the household had almost forgotten possible."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Childhood breathes again."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Connection objection: "Smartphones connect lonely teenagers to communities they could not find offline."
  • Punishment objection: "Banning phones punishes the responsible majority for the irresponsible few."
  • Usefulness objection: "The phone is also a tool for learning; you cannot only see the harm."
  • Refutation pivot: "There is connection on the platforms, and there is engineered loneliness around it. Both are real. Only one is profitable."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: return to the casino-in-a-pocket image.
  • New move: the handle goes still; the phone goes downstairs; the night holds rest, not the algorithm.
  • Final sentence: "Childhood is not a market. Stop selling it back to them by the hour."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a magazine, ~720 words) — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

The smartphone is a casino — a small, elegant, perfectly engineered casino, built to fit a pocket, open every hour of the night, lit in colours tuned by laboratories to hold the eye, and aimed with deliberate precision at the one customer in the building least equipped to walk away from the table. A fourteen-year-old does not see a casino. She sees a phone. But the architecture of the two is identical: the near-miss, the variable reward, the handle that asks to be pulled once more. Design that profits from attention is design that costs childhood, and the house, as ever, always wins.

[U — Urgency]

The numbers are not subtle. Ofcom's 2024 Children and Parents: Media Use and Attitudes research — the most authoritative UK dataset on this — found that children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online, which is almost half their waking life outside school. Every notification is a handle pulled in a child's pocket, and every win is engineered to lose: the small dopamine reward, the escalating spend of hours, the family table emptying of attention one downturned face at a time. The Online Safety Act passed in 2023 because Parliament finally recognised the machine. This is not entertainment. This is extraction.

[M1 — Design, not user]

The instinct, when something goes wrong with a child, is to regulate the child. The instinct is wrong. We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards. Q3 — Climate, sustainability, individual action

Q3 — Climate, sustainability, individual action

Why this is likely. Sustainability is one of Edexcel's recurring Section B clusters and sits inside a decade of record-breaking UK temperatures. The reading pairs with Edexcel's 19th-century natural-history and travel writing and 21st-century climate journalism. A 2026 candidate writes from inside the most climate-salient period the paper has ever set.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We have run out of someone-else-must-act. Every household, every postcode, every weekday morning is now where the climate is decided." Write a speech for a community meeting.
  2. Write a section for a guide aimed at families, advising them on practical ways to cut their household's carbon footprint.
  3. "Personal recycling and meat-free Mondays will not save the planet. The problem is corporate, not individual." Write an article for a newspaper arguing for or against.

Frame. Individual climate action without policy is performance, but policy without citizen pressure is decoration. The Sophicly position holds both at once: the household votes with its choices, but the choice that matters most is the vote at the ballot box.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Shocking statistic: "In July 2022, the United Kingdom passed forty degrees Celsius for the first time in recorded history. The tarmac softened, the railways buckled, and the country went back to work the next morning as if nothing had changed."
  • Bold statement: "There is no household innocent of the climate."
  • Imagine scenario: "Picture 2050. The summer that was once a record is now an ordinary Tuesday."
  • Topic bridge: the climate is not a crisis to feel; it is a posture to take.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: climate change as a slow flood already inside the home.
  • Metaphor: "The flood is not coming. It is in the hallway."
  • Extension: the carpet damp; the wallpaper bubbling; the family pretending nothing.
  • Evidence: Met Office 2022 (UK passed 40°C); Climate Change Committee 2024 progress report (UK off track for 2030 targets); Carbon Brief on UK consumption emissions.
  • Devices: triadic (carpet / wallpaper / family); short pivot ("This is the room.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — The household. Frame: the smallest unit of climate policy is the kitchen. Metaphor: "Your fridge is a small parliament." Develop: the meat-free week, the thermostat at nineteen, the second-hand coat. Evidence: Carbon Brief on the gap between UK production and consumption emissions.
  • M2 — The street. Frame: street-level pressure changes councils. Metaphor: "Pavements are policy waiting to happen." Develop: tree-planting petitions, school-streets schemes, community insulation co-operatives. Evidence: Transport for London's School Streets programme now operates at around 1,000 schools, closing roads to through-traffic at drop-off and pick-up, and has cut morning traffic by up to half in monitored boroughs such as Islington.
  • M3 — The ballot box. Frame: the vote is the loudest action a household can take. Metaphor: "A ballot is the household's longest lever." Develop: voting on local climate plans; pressuring MPs on the 2030 pathway. Evidence: Climate Change Committee 2024 progress report.
  • Transitions: The kitchen is one step. The street is the next. The ballot is the last.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The street has trees again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off."
  • Sentence 3 (2 words): "Britain breathes."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Corporate-only objection: "Individual action is a distraction; only corporate regulation matters."
  • Wealth objection: "Richer households can afford solar panels; the poorest cannot, so the policy is unfair."
  • Despair objection: "Britain is a small share of global emissions; nothing we do moves the needle."
  • Refutation pivot: "The corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore; the regulator answers to the voter it cannot avoid. Withdraw the consumer and the corporation moves. Withdraw the voter and the regulator vanishes. Both move together, or neither moves."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the forty-degree July returns; the radio now reads a falling number.
  • New move: the household has chosen; the street has chosen; the ballot has chosen.

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech for a community meeting, ~720 words) — opens with a shocking-statistic hook

[I — Introduction]

In July 2022, the United Kingdom passed forty degrees Celsius for the first time in recorded history. The Met Office confirmed it. The tarmac softened; the railways buckled; a runway at an RAF base partially melted; and the country, having stared briefly at a number it had never seen before, went back to work the following morning as if nothing whatever had changed. That morning is the whole problem in a single image. The climate did not fail us in 2022. We failed to treat forty degrees as the warning it was, and we have been failing to treat it as one ever since. The climate is not a crisis to feel. It is a posture to take.

[U — Urgency]

Here is the part nobody wants said aloud in a community hall: the flood is not coming. It is in the hallway. The carpet is already damp; the wallpaper is already bubbling; and the family is already standing in the kitchen pretending the water is somebody else's. The 2024 Climate Change Committee progress report told Parliament that the United Kingdom is no longer on track to meet its legally binding 2030 emissions targets — that the existing pace of decarbonisation will not get there. Carbon Brief's analysis confirms what the policy data implies: we are exporting our pollution faster than we are eliminating it. This is the room. We are standing in it.

[M1 — The household]

And yet the kitchen is also where the country is built. Your fridge is a small parliament. Three meals a day, multiplied across millions of households, produce the supply chain the nation drives. A meat-free week shifts measurable carbon out of that chain; a thermostat at nineteen rather than twenty-two lets the gas-fired power station breathe out a little less; a second-hand coat worn for a winter costs less and survives further. The household, multiplied, is policy in disguise.

[M2 — The street]

Pavements are policy waiting to happen. The school-streets schemes that close roads to traffic at the school gate, the residents' tree-planting petitions, the community insulation co-operatives that pool a neighbourhood's draughty homes into one retrofit order — these projects look, in their early weeks, like noise. They become, in their later months, the infrastructure the council is forced to recognise. Street pressure is the country's most underestimated political instrument, and it begins on the pavement outside this hall.

[M3 — The ballot box]

The ballot is the household's longest lever, and the only mechanism that can shift the country back onto the 2030 target the Climate Change Committee measures us against. The kitchen and the street do their work; the ballot box does the rest. Withdraw the voter and the regulator vanishes. The corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore; the regulator answers to the voter it cannot avoid.

[V — Vision]

The street has trees again. Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off. Insulation has stilled the draught beneath every front door. Britain breathes.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say individual action is a distraction, that only corporate regulation matters. There is truth in this. There is also the harder truth: the corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore, and the regulator answers to the voter it cannot avoid. Pull the consumer and the corporation moves; pull the voter and the regulator wakes. Both move together, or neither moves. The household is not powerless. The household is the lever the corporation hopes it never discovers.

[C — Conclusion]

So return to that morning in July 2022, the country reading forty degrees and going back to work. We can choose a different morning. We can throttle the warming from the kitchen, from the street, from the ballot box, from a thousand small simultaneous decisions repeated by millions of households every Tuesday until the curve bends. The science is patient. The decade is not. The climate listens to small rooms. Speak from yours.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (a guide section for families on cutting their carbon footprint — let the three M-beats become three usable household actions) and Stem 3 (an article disputing or defending "the problem is corporate, not individual"). The shocking-statistic opening suits the speech and the article; for the guide, open with an imagine-scenario so the practical register lands.

Q4 — Family, generational differences

Q4 — Family, generational differences

Why this is likely. The relationship between the generations is a perennial Section B cluster, well suited to a letter or a speech and primed often in Edexcel's reading, where a Victorian text on family duty pairs with a modern one on changing households. A statement-of-opinion stem on whether the generations understand each other is overdue.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Every generation believes the one beneath it has had things easier. Every generation is wrong." Write an article for a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "Older people complain that the young have no respect. The young reply that respect was never explained, only demanded." Write a speech for a debate on the generation gap.
  3. Write a section for a guide aimed at teenagers, advising them on how to understand and get along with the older members of their family.

Frame. The generation gap is not a gap in values; it is a gap in translation. Each generation grew up solving a different problem, and mistakes the other's solution for a lack of one. Understanding begins when each stops defending its answer and starts naming its question.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Historical contrast: "My grandmother left school at fifteen and was grateful for the work. I will leave school at eighteen and be told I am lucky to have the choice. We are not divided by values. We are divided by the worlds that taught us."
  • Bold statement: "There is no generation gap. There is only a translation problem nobody has agreed to solve."
  • Personal anecdote: "The argument about my phone at the dinner table was never about the phone."
  • Topic bridge: the gap is in translation, not in values.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: two generations speaking across a table in two dialects of the same language.
  • Metaphor: "We are reading each other's lives in translation, and blaming the other for the words that did not survive the crossing."
  • Extension: the grandparent who saved because there was nothing; the teenager who spends because there is everything; both certain the other has it wrong.
  • Evidence: argue from lived contrast rather than a statistic here — the grandparent shaped by post-war scarcity, the teenager shaped by abundance and a housing market neither generation chose. The concrete detail persuades more than a survey figure would.
  • Devices: triadic (the saver / the spender / the silence between them); short pivot ("This is not disrespect.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Name the world they grew up in. Frame: understand the question before you judge the answer. Metaphor: "Every habit was once a survival skill." Develop: the grandparent's thrift as a response to scarcity; the parent's caution as a response to recession.
  • M2 — Ask, do not assume. Frame: the story you have not heard is the one that explains them. Metaphor: "A family is an archive nobody has opened." Develop: the deliberate question; the recorded memory; the photograph explained.
  • M3 — Translate, do not surrender. Frame: understanding is not agreement. Metaphor: "You can learn the dialect without abandoning your own." Develop: meeting them on technology; teaching gently; conceding nothing of who you are.
  • Transitions: The world is one step. The question is the second. The translation is the third.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The dinner table goes quiet."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Not from argument now, but because someone has finally started listening."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "The crossing closes."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Effort objection: "It is the older generation's job to adapt to the young, not the other way round."
  • Irreconcilable objection: "Some values genuinely conflict; understanding cannot dissolve them."
  • Time objection: "Teenagers are too busy to sit and interview their grandparents."
  • Refutation pivot: "Understanding is not surrender, and translation is not agreement. You can hear the world that shaped a person without adopting it — and only once you have heard it can you disagree with it honestly."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the grandmother at fifteen; the writer at eighteen.
  • New move: two worlds, finally named to each other across the same table.
  • Final sentence: "Ask the older question. The answer is the family you were born into and never met."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a newspaper, ~715 words) — opens with a historical-contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

My grandmother left school at fifteen and was grateful for the work. I will leave school at eighteen and be told, more than once, that I am lucky to have the choice. Place those two sentences beside each other and the so-called generation gap dissolves into something far less dramatic and far more solvable: we were not divided by values. We were divided by the worlds that taught us. She learned thrift because there was nothing; I learned ambition because there was everything; and each of us, looking across the dinner table, mistook the other's answer for a missing question.

[U — Urgency]

This is the quiet tragedy of most families. We are reading each other's lives in translation, and blaming the other for the words that did not survive the crossing. The grandparent who saved every coin because the war had taught her to; the teenager who spends because the algorithm has taught him a different lesson entirely; the silence between them that everyone calls disrespect. It is not disrespect. It is two dialects of the same language, spoken by people who never agreed on a single dictionary, each certain the other has simply got the grammar wrong.

[M1 — Name the world they grew up in]

So begin by naming the world they grew up in. Every habit was once a survival skill. My grandmother's refusal to throw away a margarine tub is not meanness; it is a reflex installed by rationing, a small monument to a decade when waste could mean hunger. My father's nervousness about debt is not timidity; it is the scar tissue of a recession he watched take the houses of people he knew. Understand the question a habit was answering, and the habit stops looking like a fault.

[M2 — Ask, do not assume]

Then ask, rather than assume. A family is an archive nobody has opened. The grandparent across the table is carrying a story that explains almost everything you find baffling about her — but it will not volunteer itself; it has to be asked for. A deliberate question, a recorded memory, a photograph finally explained: these are the keys to a room most teenagers walk past for eighteen years. The story you have not heard is precisely the one that would translate the person you cannot understand.

[M3 — Translate, do not surrender]

And translate, rather than surrender. You can learn the dialect without abandoning your own. Meeting a grandparent on technology — the patient half-hour teaching her to video-call — is not a betrayal of your generation; it is an act of translation in the only direction that currently works. Understanding is not agreement. You can carry your own values across the table fully intact and still arrive on the other side able to hear someone who does not share them.

[V — Vision]

The dinner table goes quiet. Not from argument now, but because someone has finally started listening. The grandmother explains the margarine tub; the grandson explains the phone; neither converts the other, and both, for the first time, understand. The crossing closes.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say it is the older generation's job to adapt to the young, not the reverse. There is fairness in the complaint. But understanding is not surrender, and translation is not agreement. You can hear the world that shaped a person without adopting it — and only once you have heard it can you disagree with it honestly, rather than dismissing it as stubbornness. The work runs both ways, but it has to begin somewhere, and the young have the easier reach across.

[C — Conclusion]

So return to the two sentences I began with: my grandmother grateful for work at fifteen, and me told I am lucky at eighteen. They are not a quarrel. They are two worlds that have never been properly introduced. Ask the older question — the one the habit was answering, the one the photograph is hiding, the one the silence is keeping. The answer is the family you were born into and have somehow never met.

The historical-contrast hook fits the article and the speech; for the guide, open with the anecdote instead.

Q5 — Travel, responsible tourism

Q5 — Travel, responsible tourism

Why this is likely. Travel is a recurring Section B cluster, and the deeper take on over-tourism is current and overdue. Edexcel's 19th-century archive is rich in Victorian travel writing, which pairs with 21st-century journalism on cruise tourism, city tourism caps, and aviation emissions. A 2026 candidate writes inside a live policy debate.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Tourism is the world's largest unregulated industry, and the one quietly destroying the places it claims to love." Write an article for a magazine.
  2. Write a section for a guide aimed at young travellers, advising them how to travel responsibly without damaging the places they visit.
  3. "Stop telling teenagers to travel. The planet cannot afford their gap years." Write a letter to a national newspaper.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a magazine, ~720 words) — opens with an imagine-scenario hook

[I — Introduction]

Picture a Greek island built, over the slow centuries, for about eight hundred people. Picture its single harbour, its handful of lanes, its one road climbing to the village. Now picture three thousand passengers walking off a single cruise ship onto that island before lunch, photographing the lanes, buying the magnets, and gone by evening. That island exists. That morning happens every summer. And it is the whole argument about modern tourism compressed into one image: an industry that has, in a single generation, grown past every mechanism we built to hold it, and now wears away the very places it came to admire. Tourism is consumption with a passport.

[U — Urgency]

The famous view now waits in a queue that begins in another country. The UN World Tourism Organization recorded over 1.4 billion international tourist arrivals in 2023 — a full return to pre-pandemic peaks at the exact moment several of the most-visited cities on earth had declared themselves overwhelmed. Rents in central Lisbon, Athens and Barcelona have been priced out of locals' reach by short-stay rental platforms; restaurant menus are printed only in English; the bakery that used to be a bakery is now a shop that sells fridge magnets. This is not travel. This is the slow consumption of place by the people who came to admire it.

[M1 — The traveller]

A holiday is not a trophy. Travel slower, travel fewer times, travel closer. Eurostar reported record passenger demand in 2024, and the carbon footprint per passenger on the London-to-Paris route is a fraction of the equivalent flight. One big trip a decade — planned for, anticipated, remembered — gives a household more than five short-haul weekends a year, and the difference is not only carbon. It is the trip itself, restored to the thing it was meant to be: an event, not an entry on a calendar.

[M2 — The destination]

Cities like Venice need a turnstile, not a tide. Amsterdam's 2023–24 city-council policy banned new hotels in the centre, capped cruise-ship arrivals, and redirected tourist-tax revenue into housing for residents. These are not anti-tourism measures; they are pro-place measures. They protect the city the visitor came to see by ensuring the city is still recognisably itself by the time the visitor arrives. A capped door is not a closed door. It is the only kind of door that keeps the house standing.

[M3 — The industry]

The cruise ship is the unit of damage — a floating town that pays no local taxes and leaves little local money. Transport & Environment's 2024 research recorded that the largest cruise ships emit enormous quantities of pollutants in a single day, and that the coastal towns hosting them see a measurable per-visitor decline in retained spend the larger the ship gets. The big ship is the visible part. The regulatory loophole that lets it dock untaxed is the part that has to change.

[V — Vision]

The street is quiet on August Tuesdays. Locals walk to bakeries that still bake; rents fall to what wages carry. The island remembers itself. Place outlasts visit.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say tourism is the livelihood of millions in developing economies, and it is. That is exactly why it should not be left to collapse under its own weight. Cap the volume, raise the per-visitor levy, redistribute the proceeds to the housing the local workforce can no longer afford, and the livelihood is preserved by the very policies that look, on the surface, like restrictions. The alternative is the destination's quiet death — and the livelihood dies with it.

[C — Conclusion]

So return to the island, the harbour, the three thousand who arrive before lunch. Imagine the same harbour with the ship docking once a week rather than seven days in seven. The island's eight hundred still earn their living. The lanes still hold. The bakery is still a bakery. The view is still worth the journey, because the journey has not destroyed the view. Travel less. Travel deeper. Pay the place its rent.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (a guide section for young travellers on responsible travel — let the three M-beats become three usable habits) and Stem 3 (a letter rejecting gap-year travel on carbon grounds, re-anchoring M1 in per-traveller arithmetic). The imagine-scenario hook fits the article and the guide; for the letter, open with the shocking statistic instead.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Sandcastle / tide metaphor anchored I → U → V → Conclusion. No shows. No abstract concept nouns. No exclamation marks.

Q6 — Money, consumerism, the value of possessions

Q6 — Money, consumerism, the value of possessions

Why this is likely. Edexcel's reading regularly pairs Victorian writing on thrift and wealth with modern writing on consumer culture, priming a Section B stem on what we own and why. The cluster is well suited to an article or a speech and is current in an era of fast fashion, fast furniture, and online impulse buying.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We own more than any generation in history and value less of it than any generation before us." Write an article for a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "Stuff does not make you happy. Everyone knows it. Everyone keeps buying anyway." Write a speech for a school assembly on consumerism.
  3. Write a section for a guide aimed at teenagers, advising them how to spend their money wisely and resist the pressure to buy.

Frame. The problem is not that we own things; it is that we have confused owning with valuing. A possession bought to be replaced is not really owned at all — it is rented from the next purchase. The value of a thing lives in how long it lasts and how well it serves, not in how new it is.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Bold statement: "We own more than any generation in history, and we value less of it than any generation before us. Both of those things are our own doing."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "Globally, only around nine percent of all plastic ever produced has ever been recycled — most of what we buy outlives our interest in it by centuries." (UNEP, 2023)
  • Sensory snapshot: "The wardrobe is full. The teenager standing in front of it says, out loud, that there is nothing to wear."
  • Topic bridge: we have confused owning with valuing.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the home as a warehouse for things bought to be replaced.
  • Metaphor: "We are not owners now; we are tenants of our own possessions, renting each one from the next purchase."
  • Extension: the flat-pack shelf binned within a year; the jumper worn twice; the phone replaced while it still works.
  • Evidence: WRAP 2022 (22m pieces of furniture discarded in the UK each year); UNEP 2023 (~9% of all plastic ever produced recycled).
  • Devices: triadic (shelf / jumper / phone); short pivot ("This is not wealth.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Buy less, choose better. Frame: the first lever is quantity. Metaphor: "One good coat outlives five cheap ones and costs less doing it." Develop: cost-per-wear thinking; the second-hand default. Evidence: WRAP 2024 (UK plastic packaging on the market) as the scale of overproduction.
  • M2 — Repair before replacing. Frame: ownership includes maintenance. Metaphor: "The cobbler is the consumer's oldest ally." Develop: Repair Cafés; the menders we stopped using. Evidence: EU Right to Repair Directive (2024), a standard the UK has yet to adopt; Repair Cafés UK.
  • M3 — Resist the manufactured want. Frame: the urge to buy is engineered, not personal. Metaphor: "The advert sells you a hole, then sells you the thing to fill it." Develop: the pause before the impulse buy; unfollowing the feed that sells. Evidence: Greenpeace UK Trashed (2021) on the export and waste of what we discard.
  • Transitions: Buying better is one step. Repairing is the second. Refusing the manufactured want is the third.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (4 words): "The wardrobe holds less."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Each thing in it was chosen, repaired when it broke, and kept."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Owning means something."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Economy objection: "Consumer spending keeps people in work; buy less and jobs vanish."
  • Joy objection: "Buying things makes people happy; do not moralise about pleasure."
  • Class objection: "Telling people to buy less ignores families who already cannot afford to buy more."
  • Refutation pivot: "Buying better is not buying nothing; it is spending the same money on fewer, longer-lasting things — which keeps the maker, the mender and the second-hand shop in work, and quietly cuts the family's costs at the same time."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the full wardrobe, nothing to wear.
  • New move: the half-full wardrobe, everything to wear.
  • Final sentence: "Own less. Value it more. The difference is the only wealth that lasts."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a newspaper, ~715 words) — opens with a bold-statement hook

[I — Introduction]

We own more than any generation in history, and we value less of it than any generation before us. Both of those things are our own doing, and the second follows from the first as surely as a hangover follows a binge. A teenager today commands a quantity of possessions that would have astonished her great-grandmother — and stands in front of a wardrobe stuffed with them announcing, with complete sincerity, that there is nothing to wear. The contradiction is not hers alone. It is the contradiction of an entire economy that has quietly confused owning a thing with valuing it.

[U — Urgency]

We are not owners now. We are tenants of our own possessions, renting each one from the next purchase. WRAP reported in 2022 that the United Kingdom discards around twenty-two million pieces of furniture every year — a flat-pack shelf bought in spring and binned by winter, a jumper worn twice, a phone replaced while it still works perfectly. The United Nations Environment Programme reported in 2023 that only around nine percent of all the plastic ever produced has ever been recycled, which means most of what we buy will outlive our interest in it by centuries. This is not wealth. This is accumulation pretending to be it.

[M1 — Buy less, choose better]

The first lever is quantity. One good coat outlives five cheap ones, and costs less doing it. Cost-per-wear is the arithmetic the advertising industry hopes you never learn: a forty-pound coat worn three times costs more per wearing than a two-hundred-pound coat worn three hundred times. WRAP's 2024 data put more than two million tonnes of plastic packaging onto the UK market in a single year — the visible scale of an economy built on volume. Choose better, buy rarely, and the second-hand rail becomes the first place you look, not the last.

[M2 — Repair before replacing]

Ownership includes maintenance. The cobbler is the consumer's oldest ally, and we have nearly forgotten him. Repair Cafés UK now host hundreds of free monthly events where broken things are mended by volunteers, and the European Union's Right to Repair Directive, agreed in 2024, requires manufacturers to offer repair for years after a sale — a standard the United Kingdom has not yet adopted. Every kettle thrown out because it was cheaper to replace than to mend is a small confession that we have lost the economy of repair. Mend the thing. It is yours; act as though it is.

[M3 — Resist the manufactured want]

And resist the manufactured want, because the urge to buy is engineered, not personal. The advert sells you a hole, then sells you the thing to fill it. Greenpeace UK's Trashed report in 2021 traced where our discarded purchases actually go — exported, dumped, burnt — and the pattern reveals a culture buying faster than it can use, prompted at every scroll by a feed designed to manufacture dissatisfaction. Pause before the impulse buy. Unfollow the feed that exists to sell. The want you did not feel yesterday was installed, not inherited.

[V — Vision]

The wardrobe holds less. Each thing in it was chosen, repaired when it broke, and kept. The home stops being a warehouse and starts being a home. Owning means something.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say consumer spending keeps people in work, and that if we all buy less, jobs vanish. But buying better is not buying nothing. It is spending the same money on fewer, longer-lasting things — which keeps the maker, the mender and the second-hand shop in work, and quietly cuts the family's costs at the same time. The job that depends on a product failing within a year was never a secure job. It was a job built on waste, and waste is the one thing the next decade cannot afford to protect.

[C — Conclusion]

So return to the full wardrobe and the teenager who has nothing to wear. The cure is not a bigger wardrobe. It is a smaller one, holding fewer things, each chosen and kept and mended and meant. Own less. Value it more. The difference between the two is the only kind of wealth that has ever actually lasted.

The bold-statement hook fits the article and the speech; for the guide, open with the sensory snapshot instead.

Q7 — Mental health, pressure on young people

Q7 — Mental health, pressure on young people

Why this is likely. Adolescent wellbeing is among the most salient topics of the decade and well suited to a speech or a letter. Edexcel's reading pairs Victorian writing on nerves, work and endurance with modern writing on stress, priming a Section B stem on the pressures facing the young. A statement-of-opinion task on whether we are protecting or overloading young people is overdue.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We tell teenagers to look after their mental health, then load them with the very pressures that wreck it." Write a speech for a school assembly.
  2. "Resilience is just a word adults use to avoid fixing what is breaking young people." Write a letter to a national newspaper giving your views.
  3. Write a section for a guide aimed at students, advising them how to protect their mental health during exam season.

Frame. The pressure on young people is not a weakness in them to be toughened away; it is a load placed on them that adults rarely name. Protecting mental health is not about teaching teenagers to cope with an unreasonable load — it is about reducing the load and teaching them to carry what remains.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Extended metaphor: "We hand teenagers a rucksack, and every year we add a stone — exams, screens, comparison, the future — and then we wonder, when one of them stumbles, why they were not stronger."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "Teenagers need eight to ten hours of sleep a night, and their body clocks run one to three hours later than an adult's — so the school morning fights their biology before the pressure even begins." (NHS / Sleep Foundation; Crowley et al., Current Biology, 2019)
  • Sensory snapshot: "Sunday night. The revision timetable is colour-coded. The fifteen-year-old has not slept properly since Tuesday."
  • Topic bridge: the load is the problem, not the carrier.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the teenager as a rucksack quietly overfilled.
  • Metaphor: "We keep adding stones, then call the stumble a lack of resilience."
  • Extension: the exam volume; the always-on screen; the comparison machine; the silence about all three.
  • Evidence: NHS England's 2023 survey found one in five (20.3%) of 8–16-year-olds had a probable mental disorder, a proportion rising since 2017; NHS / Sleep Foundation (teens need 8–10 hrs); Crowley et al., Current Biology (2019) on the delayed adolescent body clock.
  • Devices: triadic (exams / screens / comparison); short pivot ("This is not weakness.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Name the load. Frame: you cannot lighten what you will not measure. Metaphor: "A stone you can see is a stone you can put down." Develop: writing down what is actually being carried; separating the urgent from the invented. Evidence: the PSHE Association provides schools with wellbeing and media-literacy guidance that teaches exactly this naming-and-sorting skill.
  • M2 — Protect the basics. Frame: sleep, movement and daylight are not luxuries; they are load-bearing. Metaphor: "You do not study harder by sleeping less; you study worse, slower, and sadder." Develop: the protected eight hours; the daily walk; the phone out of the bedroom. Evidence: NHS / Sleep Foundation; Crowley et al. (2019); Paul Kelly, University of Oxford (2019), on later school starts improving outcomes.
  • M3 — Ask for help early. Frame: the conversation is the intervention. Metaphor: "A problem said aloud is a problem already half-carried by someone else." Develop: the tutor, the parent, the GP, the helpline; naming it before crisis. Evidence: YoungMinds, the UK's leading youth mental-health charity, runs exactly these support routes — a parents' helpline, a crisis text line, and school resources.
  • Transitions: Naming the load is one step. Protecting the basics is the second. Asking early is the third.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The rucksack carries only stones."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The invented ones have been set down; the real ones are shared."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "The walk continues."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Resilience objection: "Life is hard; teenagers must learn to cope, not be coddled."
  • Standards objection: "Lowering the pressure means lowering the standards."
  • Exaggeration objection: "Every generation says it had it worst; the young are not uniquely strained."
  • Refutation pivot: "Reducing an unreasonable load is not coddling; it is the difference between training and injury. You build strength by progressive load, not by crushing weight — and a generation crushed does not learn resilience. It learns to hide the strain."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the rucksack and the added stones.
  • New move: the load named, the basics protected, the weight shared.
  • Final sentence: "Put down the stones that were never yours to carry. Then walk."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech for a school assembly, ~720 words) — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

Imagine we hand every teenager in this hall a rucksack on their first day of secondary school. It starts light. Then, every single year, we add a stone. A stone for exams. A stone for the screen that never switches off. A stone for the comparison machine in their pocket, measuring them against people who do not exist. A stone for a future nobody will stop asking them about. And then, when one of them stumbles under a weight we ourselves loaded, we lean down and ask, with genuine confusion, why they were not stronger. The load is the problem. Not the person carrying it.

[U — Urgency]

We keep adding stones, and then we call the stumble a lack of resilience. Consider the load honestly. The NHS and the Sleep Foundation agree that teenagers need between eight and ten hours of sleep a night — and research by Crowley and colleagues, published in Current Biology in 2019, confirmed that the adolescent body clock runs one to three hours later than an adult's. So the school morning is already fighting biology before the exam pressure, the screen pressure and the comparison pressure are even added. This is not weakness. This is a reasonable person carrying an unreasonable weight, and being told the weight is character-building.

[M1 — Name the load]

So the first thing is to name the load. A stone you can see is a stone you can put down. Most of the pressure a teenager carries is never written down, never separated into the urgent and the invented — it simply sits there, heavy and unsorted. Take ten minutes and list it: what is real, what is a deadline, what is a comparison that does not matter, what is a future that has not arrived. Half of any rucksack, examined honestly, turns out to be stones somebody else convinced you to carry.

[M2 — Protect the basics]

The second thing is to protect the basics, because sleep, movement and daylight are not luxuries — they are load-bearing. You do not study harder by sleeping less; you study worse, slower, and sadder. The University of Oxford's 2019 work on later school start times found measurable gains in attendance and attainment simply from letting adolescent bodies wake naturally. Protect the eight hours. Take the daily walk. Put the phone out of the bedroom, where it cannot steal the sleep that does the actual repair. The basics are not the reward for coping. They are how you cope.

[M3 — Ask for help early]

And the third thing is to ask for help early, because the conversation is the intervention. A problem said aloud is a problem already half-carried by someone else. The tutor, the parent, the GP, the helpline — none of them needs a crisis before they are allowed to help; they need a sentence, said early, before the stone becomes a boulder. The bravest thing in this hall is not silent endurance. It is the student who says, out loud, that the rucksack has become too heavy.

[V — Vision]

The rucksack carries only stones that belong in it. The invented ones have been set down; the real ones are shared. The basics — sleep, daylight, movement — hold the weight steady. The walk continues, and the walker arrives.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say life is hard, and that teenagers must learn to cope rather than be coddled. I agree that life is hard. But reducing an unreasonable load is not coddling; it is the difference between training and injury. [I — Introduction]

Imagine we hand every teenager in this hall a rucksack on their first day of secondary school. It starts light. Then, every single year, we add a stone. A stone for exams. A stone for the screen that never switches off. A stone for the comparison machine in their pocket, measuring them against people who do not exist. A stone for a future nobody will stop asking them about. And then, when one of them stumbles under a weight we ourselves loaded, we lean down and ask, with genuine confusion, why they were not stronger. The load is the problem. Not the person carrying it.

The bravest thing in this hall is not silent endurance. It is the student who says, out loud, that the rucksack has become too heavy.

The rucksack carries only stones that belong in it. The invented ones have been set down; the real ones are shared. The basics — sleep, daylight, movement — hold the weight steady. The walk continues, and the walker arrives.

Q8 — Animals, nature, conservation

Q8 — Animals, nature, conservation

Why this is likely. Animal welfare and conservation is one of Edexcel's most reliable Section B clusters, suiting an article, a speech or a guide. The reading pairs cleanly: Edexcel's 19th-century archive is rich in natural-history writing, and 21st-century wildlife journalism on rewilding, captivity, and population collapse is abundant. A 2026 candidate writes inside a live debate about how we treat animals.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Zoos are nineteenth-century institutions pretending to be twenty-first-century arks. Shut them." Write an article for a magazine.
  2. Write a section for a guide aimed at families, advising them how to help protect wildlife and nature in their everyday lives.
  3. "Conservation needs zoos. The alternative is a generation of children who never see what we are losing." Write a speech for a school assembly.

Frame. The question is not whether we love animals. We do. The question is whether the institutions we built in their name have outgrown the morality that built them — and whether real protection now lives in the field, not behind glass.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Assumption-then-reversal: "You have heard that zoos save species. You have heard it from every documentary and every charity advert. In fact, the opposite is closer to the truth."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "There are more tigers held in captivity in the United States alone than survive in the wild across the whole of Asia."
  • Sensory snapshot: "A polar bear paces three steps left, three steps right, three steps left. The glass between you is the only thing it cannot break."
  • Topic bridge: the institution outlived the ethics.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the cage as a Victorian souvenir we forgot how to throw away.
  • Metaphor: "Every enclosure is a museum exhibit of our own slow conscience."
  • Extension: the keepers know it; the children sense it; the species pace it.
  • Evidence: Born Free Foundation (captive-animal stereotypy); WWF Living Planet Report 2024 (69% average wildlife-population decline since 1970).
  • Devices: triadic (keepers / children / species); short pivot ("This is not education.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — The pacing tells the truth. Frame: stereotypic behaviour is the body of the captive animal admitting what our mouths will not. Metaphor: "The pacing is the lecture." Develop: enrichment cannot redesign a wild animal for a gift shop. Evidence: Born Free Foundation; BIAZA welfare standards.
  • M2 — Sanctuary, not spectacle. Frame: one feeds the animal first; the other sells the ticket first. Metaphor: "One is a hospital. The other is a theatre." Develop: named sanctuaries versus the safari-park business model. Evidence: Born Free rescue work; the captive-tiger figure.
  • M3 — Conservation in the wild. Frame: real conservation is field-based, slow, unglamorous. Metaphor: "A reserve is a library. A zoo is a postcard." Develop: rewilding. Evidence: Knepp rewilding (West Sussex); Yellowstone wolf reintroduction.
  • Transitions: The body speaks. The institutions choose. The field corrects.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The enclosure stands empty."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The funding has moved to the field where the animal still belongs."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Conscience caught up."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Education objection: "Children need to see real animals; otherwise conservation has no public."
  • Funding objection: "Zoos fund the conservation work nobody else pays for."
  • Endangered-species objection: "Some species now exist only in captivity; close the zoos and they vanish."
  • Refutation pivot: "A child who sees a captive tiger learns that captivity is acceptable. The lesson teaches the cage, not the species."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the polar bear's three steps left, three steps right.
  • New move: the funding moves to the field; the steps lengthen.
  • Final sentence: "Cages are an apology we are still owed. Pay it."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a magazine, ~725 words) — opens with an assumption-then-reversal hook

[I — Introduction]

You have heard that zoos save species. You have heard it every time a primary-school party files past the big-cat enclosure, every time a documentary opens with the words this rare creature can now be seen safely behind glass, every time a charity advert reminds you that without zoos the tiger would already be gone. You have heard, in short, that the institution and the conservation share a single purpose.

In fact, the opposite is closer to the truth. The zoo and the conservation share one word, and almost nothing else. Real conservation lives in the field. Captive display lives behind glass. To confuse the two is to mistake the postcard for the country it depicts.

[U — Urgency]

Every enclosure is a Victorian souvenir we have forgotten how to throw away. The keepers know it. The children sense it. The species pace it. The Born Free Foundation has documented stereotypic behaviours — the pacing, the head-bobbing, the obsessive grooming — in captive animals across British zoos for over thirty years, and the WWF Living Planet Report of 2024 records a sixty-nine percent average decline in monitored wildlife populations since 1970. Outside the cage, the species is collapsing. Inside the cage, the species is acting out the collapse. This is not education. This is exhibition.

[M1 — The pacing tells the truth]

The pacing is the lecture. Stereotypic behaviour is the body of the captive animal admitting the truth our mouths will not — that the enclosure designed for our viewing is the enclosure designed against the animal's sanity. BIAZA's welfare standards have grown more sophisticated each decade, and the better zoos have shrunk their elephant herds and dismantled their tanks. But sophistication is not absolution. An enclosure with enrichment is still an enclosure, and a tiger taught to hunt frozen mince is still a tiger redesigned for the gift shop.

[M2 — Sanctuary, not spectacle]

One is a hospital. The other is a theatre. The genuine sanctuaries — those that exist to rehabilitate and rehome, that the Born Free Foundation has built and supported across the world — feed the animal first and sell the ticket last, and they do not pretend the visit is the point. The safari park, by contrast, was designed around the family ticket: the lion sleeps where the photograph can be taken, and the gift shop opens at nine. There are more tigers in captivity in the United States alone than survive in the wild across the whole of Asia, and almost none of those captive animals will ever contribute a single cub to a wild population. The business models are not equivalent. They are opposites with the same word painted on the door.

[M3 — Conservation in the wild]

A reserve is a library. A zoo is a postcard. Real conservation is field-based, slow, and rarely glamorous: the Knepp rewilding estate in West Sussex, where land left to recover has drawn back species nobody planted; the wolf reintroduction at Yellowstone, which repaired an entire ecosystem from the top down. Each of these returns the animal to the system it evolved inside. The cage cannot do that. The cage has never done that.

[V — Vision]

The enclosure stands empty. The funding has moved to the field where the animal still belongs. Conscience caught up.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say children need to see real animals, or conservation loses its public. There is something in that. There is also this: a child who sees a captive tiger learns that captivity is acceptable. The lesson teaches the cage, not the species. The nature documentary, the school trip to a genuine reserve, the live camera feed from a real wilderness — each does what the zoo claims to do, without the body of an animal being asked to perform for the turnstile.

[C — Conclusion]

So believe, if the brochures insist, that zoos and conservation share a mission. Then look at the polar bear pacing three steps left, three steps right, three steps left, and believe whichever of the two the bear is telling you. The funding can move to the field. The enclosure can stand empty. The animal can go back to the system that made it. Cages are an apology we are still owed. Pay it.

Alternative stems. The same shell inverts for Stem 3 (a speech FOR zoos as conservation) by reframing the M-beats as field-conservation PARTNERED with visitor-facing zoos, with the Stem 1 position becoming the counter-argument. Stem 2 (a guide section for families on protecting wildlife) turns the three M-beats into three usable everyday actions. The assumption-then-reversal hook works in either direction.
