Q9 — Career, future, goals

Q9 — Career, future, goals

Why this is likely. Edexcel returns regularly to work and the future, a cluster that suits a speech, a letter or an article. The reading pairs Victorian career and apprenticeship writing with modern journalism on work, the trades, and the public services. A statement-of-opinion stem on what we should choose careers for is well overdue.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Stop asking teenagers what they want to be. Ask what they want to be for." Write a speech for a Year 11 careers evening.
  2. "Nursing pays less than retail; teaching pays less than IT support. Then we wonder why the wards are short and the staffrooms empty." Write a letter to the Minister for Education.
  3. Write a section for a guide aimed at teenagers, advising them how to choose a career that is right for them.

Frame. The country has confused status with significance. The work that holds society together — teaching, nursing, building, repairing — is the work society pretends to admire and quietly underpays. A career chosen for status is a career chosen against meaning; a career chosen for purpose tends, in the long run, to be the one that lasts.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Rhetorical question: "What do you want to be? It is the question every adult asks a sixteen-year-old, and it is the wrong one. The right question is shorter and harder: what do you want to be for?"
  • Counterintuitive fact: "A newly qualified secondary teacher in 2025 earns less, in real terms, than a newly qualified teacher did in 2010."
  • Bold statement: "Status is the country's most expensive substitute for purpose."
  • Topic bridge: the pay packet has stopped tracking the work that matters.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: a country quietly emptying out of its essential workforce.
  • Metaphor: "We are picking the fruit and forgetting to water the tree."
  • Extension: the GP surgery that cannot recruit; the school short five teachers in September; the bus cancelled for want of a driver.
  • Evidence: DfE & NHS England 2024 vacancy data; IFS on public-sector real-terms pay erosion since 2010; NFER 2023 (teacher pay below 2010 in real terms).
  • Devices: triadic (surgery / school / bus); short pivot ("This is not a labour market.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Choose for purpose, not status. Frame: the work that means something tends to outlast the work that merely pays. Metaphor: "A job chosen for the badge is a job you grow out of; a job chosen for the work is one you grow into." Develop: the vocation that holds; the prestige that fades. Evidence: Sutton Trust 2023 on careers aspirations and social mobility.
  • M2 — Pay the work that holds the country. Frame: significance should not be punished. Metaphor: "Nursing is national infrastructure; pay it like infrastructure." Develop: the case for a real-terms recovery. Evidence: IFS pay-erosion data; NFER 2023.
  • M3 — The route is not only the degree. Frame: a degree is one road, not the only one. Metaphor: "An apprenticeship is not a lower drawer; it is a different cabinet." Develop: degree-equivalent apprenticeships in engineering, healthcare, construction. Evidence: DfE / Skills England 2024 apprenticeship data (IFATE dissolved into Skills England, April 2024).
  • Transitions: Purpose is one step. Fair pay is the second. The right route is the third.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (3 words): "Wards fill again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Sixteen-year-olds choose work that means something, and the paycheque admits it."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Purpose is paid."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Money objection: "Idealism does not pay the rent; status often means survival."
  • Choice objection: "Teenagers should be free to choose status over purpose if they want to."
  • Tax objection: "Paying essential workers properly means higher taxes for everyone."
  • Refutation pivot: "Choosing purpose is not choosing poverty; it is refusing to let the badge make the decision the work should make. And paying the people who hold the country together is not a cost to the country — it is the cost OF the country, paid in salary now or in collapse later."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the question "what do you want to be?"
  • New move: the better question — "what do you want to be for?"
  • Final sentence: "Choose what you want to be for. The rest is just the badge."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech for a Year 11 careers evening, ~720 words) — opens with a rhetorical-question hook

[I — Introduction]

What do you want to be? It is the question every adult in your life has asked you since you were small, and tonight I want to suggest, with respect to all of them, that it is the wrong question. It treats a career as a costume — something you put on, a label you wear, a word you give at parties. The right question is shorter and a great deal harder, and it is this: what do you want to be for? Not what title do you want, but what work do you want your one life to do. Answer the second question, and the first one tends to answer itself.

[U — Urgency]

I ask because, right now, the country is getting the answer badly wrong. We are picking the fruit and forgetting to water the tree. Across Britain, the GP surgery cannot recruit. The new-build school opens in September short five teachers. The bus you took here this evening may have been cancelled at six this morning because the depot had no driver. Department for Education and NHS England vacancy data from 2024 confirm what every household already suspects: the work that holds this country together is the work this country has quietly stopped paying for. This is not a labour market. This is an emptying.

[M1 — Choose for purpose, not status]

So choose for purpose, not for status. A job chosen for the badge is a job you grow out of; a job chosen for the work is one you grow into. The Sutton Trust has reported for years that teenagers receive far more encouragement toward prestige than toward meaning, and almost none toward the vocational careers that hold communities together. The prestige fades. The status that dazzled you at sixteen will bore you at thirty. The work that means something does the opposite — it deepens. Pick the work, not the word people will use for you at parties.

[M2 — Pay the work that holds the country]

And as a country, we have to pay the work that holds it. Significance should not be punished. A newly qualified secondary teacher in 2025 earns less, in real terms, than a teacher did in 2010 — the Institute for Fiscal Studies and the National Foundation for Educational Research have both confirmed the erosion across more than a decade. Nursing is national infrastructure; we should pay it like infrastructure. Teaching is national infrastructure; we should pay it like infrastructure. The Treasury that will not fund a classroom this year pays, before the decade is out, for the classroom that never opened.

[M3 — The route is not only the degree]

And remember that the route is not only the degree. An apprenticeship is not a lower drawer; it is a different cabinet. Department for Education and Skills England data — published since Skills England absorbed the old apprenticeship body in April 2024 — record degree-equivalent apprenticeships in engineering, healthcare and construction matching or exceeding graduate-route earnings within a few years. The country needs the routes, and the routes pay for the country. Refuse the quiet snobbery that still tells capable sixteen-year-olds the apprenticeship is the second-best decision.

[V — Vision]

Wards fill again. Sixteen-year-olds choose work that means something, and the paycheque admits it. Engineering desks fill; construction sites take their own apprentices. Purpose is paid.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you, sensibly, are thinking that idealism does not pay the rent — that status often means survival. I am not asking you to choose poverty. Choosing purpose is not choosing poverty; it is refusing to let the badge make the decision the work should make. And paying the people who hold the country together is not a cost to the country. It is the cost of the country, paid in salary now or in collapse later. The bill arrives either way. The only question is the currency.

[C — Conclusion]

So I will end with the question I began with, corrected. Tomorrow, an adult will ask you what you want to be, and you will feel the old pull toward the impressive answer. Resist it. Ask yourself the harder version instead — what do you want to be for — and let the work, not the word, decide. Choose what you want to be for. The rest is just the badge.

The same shell answers Stem 2 (a letter to the Minister for Education on pay) and Stem 3 (a guide section for teenagers on choosing the right career — let the three M-beats become three usable steps). The rhetorical-question hook fits the speech especially well; for the letter, open with the counterintuitive fact instead.

Q10 — Discrimination, equality, fairness

Q10 — Discrimination, equality, fairness

Why this is likely. Fairness and equality is one of the enduring Section B clusters and pairs naturally with Edexcel's reading, where a Victorian text on social injustice meets a modern one on equality. A statement-of-opinion stem on how we judge and exclude people is well suited to an article, a speech or a letter, and is overdue.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We have laws against discrimination and we still discriminate every single day — quietly, by habit, without noticing." Write an article for a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "People should be judged on what they do, not on how they look, where they are from, or who they love." Write a speech for a school assembly.
  3. Write a section for a guide aimed at students, advising them how to recognise and challenge unfairness in everyday life.

Frame. Discrimination is rarely the dramatic act the law was written to catch. It is the small, automatic judgement — the assumption made in a second, the door not held, the chance not offered. Fairness is not the absence of hatred; it is the active, daily work of noticing the judgements we make before we have decided to make them.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Repetition / anaphora: "We measure people before they speak. We compare them before we know them. We exclude them before we have given them a single reason to be included. And we tell ourselves, all the while, that we are fair."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "More than a decade after the Equality Act, the Equality and Human Rights Commission still records a disability pay gap of nearly 13 per cent and an employment gap of more than 30 percentage points. The law changed; the habit did not." (EHRC, Equality and Human Rights Monitor, 2023.)
  • Bold statement: "The law against discrimination is decades old. The habit of it is older, and stronger."
  • Topic bridge: discrimination is the small automatic judgement, not only the dramatic act.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the snap judgement as a door quietly closing before the person reaches it.
  • Metaphor: "We sort people in a heartbeat, then call the sorting common sense."
  • Extension: the name skipped on the CV; the accent corrected; the assumption that arrives before the person.
  • Evidence: the EHRC's 2023 Monitor recorded a disability pay gap of 12.7% and a disability employment gap of around 32 percentage points; the Equality Act 2010 is the legal floor that habit still ducks under.
  • Devices: triadic (the CV / the accent / the assumption); short pivot ("This is not fairness.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — Notice the snap judgement. Frame: you cannot challenge what you do not catch. Metaphor: "The fastest thought is the one that needs checking most." Develop: the deliberate pause; questioning the first impression. Evidence: psychologists Greenwald and Banaji, who developed the Implicit Association Test, argue that people form automatic associations about others faster than conscious thought — though, in fairness, researchers continue to debate how reliably such tests predict real behaviour, so write "research suggests" rather than "research proves".
  • M2 — Open the door you would have closed. Frame: fairness is an action, not a feeling. Metaphor: "Inclusion is a door held, not a sentiment felt." Develop: the chance offered; the name read fairly; the voice invited in. Evidence: the Equality Act 2010 as the floor, with everyday action as the building above it.
  • M3 — Speak when it is not about you. Frame: silence in the face of unfairness is a vote for it. Metaphor: "The bystander writes the ending." Develop: naming the joke that was not funny; backing the person who was talked over. Evidence: argue from principle here — the bystander who stays silent endorses the unfairness by default; the one who speaks changes the room.
  • Transitions: Noticing is one step. Opening the door is the second. Speaking up is the third.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The door is held open."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The name is read fairly; the voice that was talked over is heard."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Fairness becomes ordinary."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Oversensitivity objection: "People see discrimination everywhere now; some of it is just life."
  • Law-is-enough objection: "We already have equality laws; the problem is solved."
  • Free-thought objection: "You cannot police what people think in the privacy of their own heads."
  • Refutation pivot: "Nobody is policing the thought; the work is noticing it before it becomes the action. The law sets the floor, and the floor matters — but a country does not become fair by obeying the minimum. It becomes fair in the small daily choices the law cannot reach."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the measuring, the comparing, the excluding.
  • New move: the noticing, the opening, the speaking.
  • Final sentence: "Fairness is not a law we passed once. It is a habit we choose every day. Choose it."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a newspaper, ~720 words) — opens with a repetition / anaphora hook

[I — Introduction]

We measure people before they speak. We compare them before we know them. We exclude them before we have given them a single reason to be included. And we tell ourselves, all the while, that we are fair — that fairness is something we settled long ago, written into law, filed away, done. It is not done. The dramatic discrimination that the law was written to catch is the smallest part of the problem. The larger part is the judgement made in a heartbeat, by ordinary people who would be genuinely wounded to be called unfair, in the second before they have decided anything at all.

[U — Urgency]

We sort people in a heartbeat, then call the sorting common sense. The name skipped on the CV because it looked unfamiliar. The accent quietly marked down in the interview. The assumption that arrives a full second before the person it is about. The Equality Act of 2010 set a legal floor against the worst of this, and the floor matters — but habit has spent fifteen years learning to duck underneath it, into the territory no law can reach: the glance, the doubt, the door that closes before the person is even near it. This is not fairness. This is prejudice that has learned to be quiet.

[M1 — Notice the snap judgement]

So the first work is to notice the snap judgement, because you cannot challenge what you do not catch. The fastest thought is the one that needs checking most. The impression that forms before someone has spoken — about their accent, their clothes, their name, their face — is precisely the impression to distrust, because it was assembled too quickly to have used any actual evidence. A deliberate pause, a moment of asking where did that come from, is not weakness. It is the only thing standing between an instinct and an injustice.

[M2 — Open the door you would have closed]

The second work is to open the door you would otherwise have closed, because fairness is an action, not a feeling. Inclusion is a door held, not a sentiment felt. It is the chance offered to the candidate whose CV you almost skipped; the name read aloud as carefully as any other; the quieter voice in the room actively invited in rather than left to fend for itself. Nobody is made fair by feeling warmly about equality in the abstract. People are made fair by what they do in the specific, repeated, unglamorous moment.

[M3 — Speak when it is not about you]

And the third work is to speak when it is not about you, because silence in the face of unfairness is a vote for it. The bystander writes the ending. The joke that was not funny, the colleague talked over, the assumption stated aloud as though it were obvious — each of these survives only as long as the room lets it. To name it, calmly, in the moment, is uncomfortable; it is also the single most powerful thing a fair person can do, precisely because it costs them nothing they cannot afford and protects someone who cannot protect themselves.

[V — Vision]

The door is held open. The name is read fairly; the voice that was talked over is heard. The CV is judged on the work, the candidate on the answers, the person on the person. Fairness becomes ordinary.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say people now see discrimination everywhere, and that some of what is called unfairness is simply life. There is a real risk of crying wolf, and it should be taken seriously. But nobody is policing the thought; the work is noticing it before it becomes the action. The law sets the floor, and the floor matters — yet a country does not become fair by obeying the minimum. It becomes fair in the thousand small daily choices the law was never able to reach.

[C — Conclusion]

So return to the three things we do without noticing: we measure, we compare, we exclude. They can be replaced, one habit at a time, by three better ones: we notice, we open the door, we speak. Fairness is not a law we passed once and may now forget. It is a habit we choose, or fail to choose, every single day. Choose it.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (a speech on judging people by what they do) and Stem 3 (a guide section for students on recognising and challenging unfairness — let the three M-beats become three usable steps). The repetition hook carries cleanly across all three; for the speech, you may shorten the anaphora to a single triad.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Door / snap-judgement metaphor anchored I → U → V → Conclusion. No shows. No exclamation marks. No patriarchy / patriarchal (Sophicly content uses society / social order / commercial pressure substitutes). EHRC 2023 inequality figures verified; unconscious-bias evidence hedged ("research suggests").

The ten plans above share underlying moves. The same metaphor frame, hook archetype, or refutation pivot reappears across multiple Qs. Memorise the recombinations and you can rebuild any plan from any other on the day.

If exam time is tight, rehearse Q2, Q3, and Q7 first — the three with the highest 2026 salience. For broader coverage rehearse all ten. The shells are deliberately recombinant: on the day, the worst case is that Edexcel prints a stem you have not rehearsed; the best case is that the metaphor frame you rehearsed for Q3 fits the Q8 stem Edexcel actually printed. Both scenarios are designed for.

Creative stretches — one extreme example per Q

The ten skeletons above are the SAFE shape — IUMVCC, six beats, the locked structure that consistently scores AO5 7+. They will land your answer firmly in Grade 8.

To push into Grade 9 / 9, the examiner needs to see voice. Not just techniques — voice. The examples in The Complete Writer's Craft show the range: a conversational, self-interrupting essay that thinks aloud and is rigorously argued beneath the loose surface; a vivid, energetic description that builds one long controlled image; an argument made entirely through contrast. One Sophicly student once wrote a film review of The Hulk* in The Hulk's own voice — the inventive form was a perfect AO5 reward, because the form FIT the topic. None of these is a template to copy. They are proof that the opening can be anything, provided it earns its place.

The rule for breaking the rules. A creative stretch only works if the voice or form choice fits the topic. The Hulk voice can review a blockbuster; it cannot mourn a funeral or write to your headteacher. Always ask: does this voice ARGUE the topic, or does it just decorate it? If the voice argues, ship it. If it decorates, cut it. Never reach for a clever voice because it impressed you somewhere else — reach for the one this question needs.

Below, one stretch idea per Q. Use them as inspiration; do NOT mimic them verbatim.

Q1 — Volunteering: write as a letter from the community itself

Stretch idea. Write the speech or article as a letter written by your town to its teenagers. The town introduces itself — its lunch club, its library, its under-elevens team — and explains, plainly, where it is fraying and where one repeated hour would hold it together. The town does not beg; it simply describes what it is, and what it could be.

Why it works. Personifying the community (apostrophe — a top-band AO5 technique) turns the abstract noun "community" into a body with a voice. The argument arrives through what the town can see about itself.

Risk to manage. Keep the town's voice plain and dignified, not sentimental. The moment it pleads, the conceit collapses. Let the specific detail — the empty driver's seat, the dark library window — do the persuading.

Q2 — Technology: write the first half as a notification feed

Stretch idea. Open the article with a stream of phone notifications across a single night — 23:41, 23:58, 00:14, 01:02 — each one small, each one harmless, the timestamps creeping later. Then the human voice arrives: "Forty notifications. One sleeping child. Whose night was that, really?"

Why it works. The form demonstrates the argument before stating it: the reader watches the night get stolen in real time. The escalation does the persuading.

Risk to manage. The feed must stay short and the timestamps must do the work. If it runs past a dozen lines it becomes a gimmick. Cut to the human voice while the device still holds the reader.

Q3 — Climate: write as a 2050 weather forecast

Stretch idea. Frame the speech as a BBC weather forecast read in 2050. The forecaster reads out forty-one degrees in Manchester, forty-three in London, in a calm professional voice — and the audience slowly realises the forecast is routine. The bathos does the moral work: alongside the catastrophic numbers, the forecaster reminds viewers to peg the washing somewhere shady.

Why it works. Speech is the locked form. The forecast persona makes the abstract concrete, and the bathos lands the point in a single line: the country has normalised the unsurvivable.

Risk to manage. The bathos must SERVE the argument, not undermine it. End by breaking character: "Or we could choose a different forecast. We still have time to write the script of a different decade."

Q4 — Family: write as a single dinner table across the decades

Stretch idea. Personify the family dining table. Let it narrate the meals it has held across three generations — the rationing meal, the prosperity meal, the silent-phone meal — and what it has watched the family stop saying to each other. The table notices the translation that nobody is making.

Why it works. The object-narrator makes "the generation gap" physical. The same table; three different families sitting at it; the argument arriving through what the table has seen.

Risk to manage. Keep the table observational, not preachy. It watches; it does not lecture. The human detail carries the point.

Q5 — Travel: write as the destination itself

Stretch idea. Personify an over-visited place — a Greek island, or Venice. Write the article as a letter FROM the place TO the world. "I have been here for fifteen centuries. I am sinking, and I am also drowning in your queue. Please choose one." Sustain the first-person voice through every IUMVCC beat.

Why it works. Apostrophe is a top-band AO5 technique. Personifying the place stops "over-tourism" being an abstract noun and makes it a body — and bodies persuade.

Risk to manage. Sustained personification is a marathon. The student voice cannot leak in. Stay in the place's voice for the full piece, breaking only at the final call to action.

Q6 — Consumerism: write as the wardrobe

Stretch idea. First-person voice of an overstuffed wardrobe. It describes everything crammed inside it — the jumper worn twice, the trousers with the tag still on, the coat bought to replace a coat that still worked — and it is tired, quietly, of being mistaken for wealth.

Why it works. Persona-shift to the object of the lie makes the household see itself from the outside. The wardrobe's weariness is the indictment.

Risk to manage. Keep the wardrobe's voice dry and observational, not whimsical. Closer to a bored witness than a children's-book narrator.

Q7 — Mental health: write as the rucksack

Stretch idea. Personify the rucksack from the safe skeleton. Let it narrate a single school year, recording each stone it is handed — exams, screens, comparison, the future — and growing heavier with each term. By the final entry it is simply too heavy to lift, and it wonders, plainly, why nobody ever asked what was inside it.

Why it works. The conceit makes the central metaphor literal. The rucksack's quiet accounting of its own weight is more damning than any statistic.

Risk to manage. The rucksack must still THINK — it must build toward the call to action implicit in its own overloading. Do not let the conceit become cute.

Q8 — Animals: write a parody Victorian zoo brochure

Stretch idea. Open the article as a verbatim 1890s brochure advertising a new zoo enclosure to the "discerning gentleman traveller" — three paragraphs of period-perfect Victorian copy. Then the modern voice cuts in: "This is not a parody. This is the brochure they printed. We are still operating the institution it sold."

Why it works. Edexcel's 19th-century paired-source archive is full of Victorian writing — the pastiche is in conversation with the paper's own DNA. The shift from period voice to modern voice IS the argument: nothing has changed except the embarrassment.

Risk to manage. Period pastiche must sound authentic for two paragraphs minimum. Read one Victorian advertisement before the exam to land the register.

Q9 — Career: write the opening as two job adverts read aloud

Stretch idea. Structure the opening as two job adverts side by side. The first: "Wanted: someone to hold a ward together through a night shift. Pay: less, in real terms, than in 2010. Reward: a life that mattered." The second: "Wanted: someone to move money between accounts that already have too much of it. Pay: more than you can spend. Reward: to be decided." Then break into the IUMVCC argument.

Why it works. The paired-advert structure makes the contrast the whole country lives inside instantly visible — the same labour market, two incompatible value systems, read in one breath.

Risk to manage. The adverts must stay tight and parallel. If the joke runs long it becomes a sketch, not an argument. Two short adverts, then out.

Q10 — Discrimination: write as the second before the decision

Stretch idea. Slow down a single heartbeat. Write the piece as a freeze-frame of the second before someone judges someone else — the CV in the hand, the eye landing on the unfamiliar name, the thought forming. Narrate that one second in extreme close-up, then let it play out twice: once as the judgement we usually make, once as the choice we could make instead.

Why it works. The form makes the argument's central claim physical — that discrimination lives in a moment too fast to notice — by stretching that moment until the reader can finally see inside it.

Risk to manage. The two endings must be genuinely parallel, the same second resolved two ways. If the contrast blurs, the device fails. Keep both versions tight and matched.

How to use these stretches in the exam

  • Do not attempt a stretch unless you have rehearsed it once. Cold improvisation under exam pressure is risky.
  • The IUMVCC beats stay. Even the most inventive persona piece still has an introduction, an argument, a counter-argument, and a conclusion. The persona or voice is the surface; the structure underneath is unchanged.
  • One stretch only. Do not try to be the wardrobe AND the rucksack AND the town. Pick one frame; sustain it.
  • If the topic does not fit, ship the safe skeleton. A bland Grade-8 answer beats a confused Grade-7 one every single time.

Final pre-submission checklist

Before you put the pen down, run this checklist in your head. Every item costs marks if it slips.

  • ✓ Six IUMVCC beats present and named in order.
  • ✓ Word count 650 or more. Under 650 risks an AO5 penalty; over 850 and control begins to slip.
  • ✓ You answered ONE task only — Q8 OR Q9, not both.
  • ✓ The form Edexcel printed is the form you wrote (article / letter / speech / review / guide section) — register matched.
  • ✓ Hook from one of the eight archetypes — not a generic opening.
  • ✓ One extended metaphor anchored across at least three beats (I → U → Conclusion at minimum).
  • ✓ Triadic structure used with parallel grammar in U and Conclusion at minimum.
  • ✓ Conclusion echoes the Introduction image (circular structure rewards AO5).
  • ✓ Direct address (you, we, us) used for solidarity, not scattered mechanically.
  • ✓ Every statistic is real. If you are unsure of a number, make the claim without the number rather than inventing one.
  • ✗ No shows / showing / shown.
  • ✗ No abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to).
  • ✗ No discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly, Secondly, Finally).
  • ✗ No really, very, just, basically.
  • ✗ No Americanisms (move as noun, gotten, trash).
  • ✗ No exclamation marks outside quoted statements.
  • ✗ Fewer than ten to be verbs (is, are, was, were).
  • ✗ No more than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.

Tick every line. The exam is forty marks. The pen is the only thing between you and them.
